content:
# Luke Chapter 10 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 99-100 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_010_page_001.jpg, LUK_010_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And after these [things], the Lord appointed seventy-two others, and sent them two [by] two before his face into every city and place where he himself was about to come.

**2.** And he was saying to them, "The harvest [is] indeed much, but the workers [are] few; therefore, ask the Lord of the harvest that he might send out workers into his harvest.

**3.** Go; behold, I send you as lambs in [the] midst of wolves.

**4.** Carry no purse, no bag, no sandals; and greet no one along the way.

**5.** And into whatever house you enter, first say, 'Peace to this house.'

**6.** And if a son of peace is there, your peace shall rest upon him; but if indeed not, it shall return upon you.

**7.** And in that same house remain, eating and drinking the [things] from them; for the worker [is] worthy of his wages. Do not move from house to house.

**8.** And into whatever city you enter and they receive you, eat the [things] being set before you,

**9.** and heal the [ones] being sick in it, and say to them, 'The kingdom of God has drawn near upon you.'

**10.** But into whatever city you enter and they do not receive you, having gone out into its streets, say,

**11.** 'Even the dust having clung to us from your city to our feet we wipe off against you; nevertheless, know this, that the kingdom of God has drawn near.'

**12.** I say to you that for Sodom in that day it shall be more tolerable than for that city.

**13.** Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if in Tyre and Sidon had been done the mighty-works having been done in you, long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes, they would have repented.

**14.** Nevertheless, for Tyre and Sidon it shall be more tolerable in the judgment than for you.

**15.** And you, Capernaum, shall you be exalted unto heaven? Unto Hades you shall be brought down.

**16.** The [one] hearing you hears me, and the [one] rejecting you rejects me; and the [one] rejecting me rejects the [One] having sent me."

**17.** And the seventy-two returned with joy, saying, "Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name."

**18.** And he said to them, "I was watching Satan, as lightning, having fallen out of the heaven.

**19.** Behold, I have given to you the authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions, and upon all the power of the enemy, and nothing shall by any means harm you.

**20.** Nevertheless, in this do not rejoice, that the spirits are subject to you; but rejoice that your names have been written in the heavens."

**21.** In that same hour he rejoiced in the Holy Spirit and said, "I confess-praise to you, Father, Lord of the heaven and of the earth, that you hid these [things] from [the] wise and understanding [ones] and revealed them to infants. Yes, Father, because thus it became well-pleasing before you.

**22.** All [things] were delivered to me by my Father, and no one knows who the Son is except the Father, and who the Father is except the Son, and [he] to whom the Son wills to reveal [him]."

**23.** And having turned to the disciples, privately he said, "Blessed [are] the eyes seeing what you see.

**24.** For I say to you that many prophets and kings wished to see what you see, and did not see [it], and to hear what you hear, and did not hear [it]."

**25.** And behold, a certain law-expert stood up, testing him, saying, "Teacher, having done what, shall I inherit eternal life?"

**26.** And he said to him, "In the law, what has been written? How do you read [it]?"

**27.** And he, having answered, said, "You shall love [the] Lord your God out of your whole heart and with your whole soul and with your whole strength and with your whole mind, and your neighbor as yourself."

**28.** And he said to him, "You answered rightly; do this, and you shall live."

**29.** But he, wishing to justify himself, said to Jesus, "And who is my neighbor?"

**30.** Having taken [this] up, Jesus said, "A certain man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and fell among robbers, who, having both stripped him and having laid on blows, went away, leaving [him] half-dead.

**31.** And by coincidence, a certain priest was going down by that way, and having seen him, he passed by on the opposite side.

**32.** And likewise also a Levite, having come upon the place, having come [and] having seen, passed by on the opposite side.

**33.** But a certain Samaritan, traveling, came upon him, and having seen [him], was moved-with-compassion.

**34.** And having approached, he bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine; and having set him upon his own animal, he brought him to an inn and took care of him.

**35.** And on the next [day], having taken out two denarii, he gave [them] to the innkeeper and said, 'Take care of him, and whatever you spend in addition, I, upon my returning, will repay to you.'

**36.** Which of these three seems to you to have become a neighbor to the [one] having fallen among the robbers?"

**37.** And he said, "The [one] having done the mercy with him." And Jesus said to him, "Go, and you yourself do likewise."

**38.** And it came to pass, in their going, he himself entered into a certain village; and a certain woman, by name Martha, received him into her house.

**39.** And she had a sister called Mary, who also, having sat at the feet of the Lord, was hearing his word.

**40.** But Martha was being distracted about much serving; and having stood over [him], she said, "Lord, does it not concern you that my sister has left me alone to serve? Tell her therefore that she should help me."

**41.** And having answered, the Lord said to her, "Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many [things];

**42.** but one [thing] is needful. For Mary chose the good part, which shall not be taken away from her."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **10:1 — "seventy-two" (εβδομηκοντα δυο):** Sinaiticus reads εβδομηκοντα δυο ("seventy-two"). This is a major textual variant: many manuscripts, including Alexandrinus, read εβδομηκοντα ("seventy"). The number seventy-two may allude to the seventy-two elders of Israel (six from each tribe, Exodus 24:1 LXX variant) or the seventy-two nations of Genesis 10 (LXX). The Sinaiticus reading "seventy-two" is supported by Vaticanus (B) and Papyrus 75, forming a strong early Alexandrian witness.

2. **10:1 — "appointed" (ανεδειξεν):** Sinaiticus reads ανεδειξεν, from αναδεικνυμι ("to show forth/appoint/designate publicly"). This is a different verb from the simple "chose" (εξελεξατο) used for the Twelve in 6:13. The term carries connotations of public installation into office, used again in Acts 1:24 for the selection of Matthias.

3. **10:1 — "others" (ετερους):** Sinaiticus reads ετερους ("others of a different kind"), not αλλους ("others of the same kind"). This may distinguish the Seventy-two as a different category of emissary from the Twelve sent in 9:1-6, though the distinction between ετερος and αλλος is not always maintained in Koine Greek.

4. **10:4 — "no sandals" (μη υποδηματα):** Sinaiticus reads μη υποδηματα, prohibiting sandals. This differs from 9:3 (the sending of the Twelve), which does not mention sandals, and from Mark 6:9, which permits them. The prohibition suggests urgency — the mission cannot wait even for proper outfitting.

5. **10:6 — "son of peace" (υιος ειρηνης):** Sinaiticus reads υιος ειρηνης, a Semitic idiom meaning "one characterized by peace" or "one destined for peace." The Hebrew construct ben-shalom underlies this expression. It describes a person receptive to the messianic peace being offered, not merely a peaceful person.

6. **10:11 — "having clung" (κολληθεντα):** Sinaiticus reads τον κονιορτον τον κολληθεντα ημιν εκ της πολεως υμων ("the dust having clung to us from your city"). The aorist passive participle κολληθεντα (from κολλαω, "to glue/cling") is more vivid than the simple "dust of your city" — even involuntary contamination is repudiated.

7. **10:13 — "Woe to you, Chorazin" (ουαι σοι Χοραζιν):** Sinaiticus reads Χοραζιν. This city is mentioned only here and in the Matthean parallel (Matt 11:21) in all of Scripture. No mighty works of Jesus in Chorazin are recorded in any Gospel, suggesting that the Gospels contain only a fraction of Jesus' deeds (cf. John 21:25).

8. **10:15 — "shall you be exalted" (μη εως ουρανου υψωθηση):** Sinaiticus reads the rhetorical question with μη expecting a negative answer: "You will not be exalted to heaven, will you?" This echoes Isaiah 14:13-15 LXX, the oracle against the king of Babylon. The descent to Hades reverses Capernaum's presumed exaltation.

9. **10:17 — "seventy-two" (εβδομηκοντα δυο):** Sinaiticus again reads "seventy-two," consistent with verse 1. Some manuscripts that read "seventy" in verse 1 also read "seventy" here, maintaining internal consistency for each textual tradition.

10. **10:18 — "having fallen" (πεσοντα):** Sinaiticus reads πεσοντα, the aorist participle of πιπτω ("to fall"), not the present participle πιπτοντα ("falling"). The aorist suggests a completed, decisive fall — not a gradual process. This vision of Satan's fall may refer to a primordial event, to the effect of the disciples' mission, or to an eschatological reality breaking into the present.

11. **10:19 — "I have given" (δεδωκα):** Sinaiticus reads δεδωκα, the perfect active indicative of διδωμι. The perfect tense indicates a gift already granted with continuing effect — the authority stands permanently. Some manuscripts read the aorist εδωκα, which would simply report the past act of giving.

12. **10:20 — "have been written" (εγγραφη):** Sinaiticus reads εγγραφη (aorist passive), though many witnesses read εγγεγραπται (perfect passive, "have been and remain written"). The concept of names written in heavenly books derives from Exodus 32:32-33 and Daniel 12:1. The passive voice implies divine authorship of the register.

13. **10:21 — "rejoiced in the Holy Spirit" (ηγαλλιασατο τω πνευματι τω αγιω):** Sinaiticus reads τω πνευματι τω αγιω ("in the Holy Spirit"), with the double article emphasizing "the Spirit, the Holy [One]." This is one of the rare instances where Jesus' emotional state is explicitly described. The verb αγαλλιαω denotes exuberant, leaping joy — not mild satisfaction.

14. **10:21 — "I confess-praise to you" (εξομολογουμαι σοι):** Sinaiticus reads εξομολογουμαι, a compound verb (εξ + ομολογεω) meaning both "to confess openly" and "to praise/give thanks." The LXX regularly uses this verb to render the Hebrew hodah ("praise/thank"). The translation "confess-praise" preserves the dual sense: acknowledgment and adoration.

15. **10:21 — "it became well-pleasing before you" (ευδοκια εγενετο εμπροσθεν σου):** Sinaiticus reads ευδοκια εγενετο εμπροσθεν σου, literally "good pleasure came to be before you." This Semitic phrasing (cf. Hebrew ratson lifne) avoids directly making God the grammatical subject. The divine will is expressed as something that "came to be" in God's presence — a reverential circumlocution.

16. **10:22 — "All things were delivered to me" (παντα μοι παρεδοθη):** Sinaiticus reads παντα μοι παρεδοθη υπο του πατρος μου. This verse, nearly identical in Matthew 11:27, is sometimes called the "Johannine thunderbolt" for its resemblance to the mutual-knowledge language of the Fourth Gospel (cf. John 10:15). The aorist passive παρεδοθη ("were delivered") suggests a definite moment of transfer.

17. **10:22 — "no one knows who the Son is except the Father":** Sinaiticus reads ουδεις γινωσκει τις εστιν ο υιος ει μη ο πατηρ. The verb γινωσκει (present active) denotes ongoing, experiential knowledge — not merely factual awareness. The reciprocal exclusivity of Father-Son knowledge is a profound Christological claim embedded in the Synoptic tradition.

18. **10:25 — "law-expert" (νομικος):** Sinaiticus reads νομικος, a term Luke prefers (6 times) over Mark's γραμματευς ("scribe"). A νομικος was specifically an expert in Torah interpretation, while a γραμματευς was more broadly a professional scribe. Luke's usage may reflect a more precise social description.

19. **10:27 — The fourfold love-command:** Sinaiticus reads εξ ολης της καρδιας σου και εν ολη τη ψυχη σου και εν ολη τη ισχυι σου και εν ολη τη διανοια σου. The four terms — heart (καρδια), soul (ψυχη), strength (ισχυς), and mind (διανοια) — expand the Shema of Deuteronomy 6:5 (LXX has three terms). The prepositions shift from εξ ("out of") for heart to εν ("with/in") for the remaining three.

20. **10:29 — "wishing to justify himself" (θελων δικαιωσαι εαυτον):** Sinaiticus reads θελων δικαιωσαι εαυτον. The verb δικαιοω here means "to show oneself righteous" or "to vindicate oneself." The lawyer's question "who is my neighbor?" seeks to limit the obligation — to define a boundary beyond which the love-command does not extend. Jesus' parable explodes this boundary.

21. **10:30 — "fell among robbers" (λησταις περιεπεσεν):** Sinaiticus reads λησταις, the term for violent brigands or bandits (not κλεπταις, "thieves"). The Jericho road descended approximately 3,400 feet over 17 miles through desolate, rocky terrain — notorious in antiquity for banditry. Strabo and Josephus both attest to the danger of this route.

22. **10:31 — "by coincidence" (κατα συγκυριαν):** Sinaiticus reads κατα συγκυριαν, a hapax legomenon in the New Testament (occurring only here). The word derives from συγκυρεω ("to happen together"). While it means "by chance" from the human perspective, the careful narrative construction suggests divine providence arranging the encounters.

23. **10:32 — "a Levite" (Λευιτης):** Sinaiticus reads Λευιτης. The narrative progression — priest, then Levite — follows the temple hierarchy. Both are cultic functionaries who might have feared corpse-defilement (Numbers 19:11-22), though this excuse is undermined by their traveling away from Jerusalem (downward), not toward it.

24. **10:33 — "was moved-with-compassion" (εσπλαγχνισθη):** Sinaiticus reads εσπλαγχνισθη, from σπλαγχνιζομαι, a verb derived from σπλαγχνα ("intestines/bowels"). This denotes a visceral, gut-level compassion. In the Synoptic Gospels, this verb is used almost exclusively of Jesus (Mk 1:41, 6:34, 8:2; Mt 9:36, 14:14, 20:34; Lk 7:13, 15:20). Its application to the Samaritan aligns his response with the compassion of God.

25. **10:34 — "pouring on oil and wine" (επιχεων ελαιον και οινον):** Sinaiticus reads επιχεων ελαιον και οινον. Oil and wine were standard medicinal treatments in antiquity. The Mishnah (Shabbat 19.2) attests to their use for wound care. Oil soothes and wine acts as an antiseptic. Isaiah 1:6 uses similar imagery for Israel's unbound wounds.

26. **10:35 — "two denarii" (δυο δηναρια):** Sinaiticus reads δυο δηναρια. A denarius was a day's wage for a laborer (cf. Matt 20:2). Two denarii could cover approximately two weeks' lodging at an inn, indicating the Samaritan's substantial generosity. The promise to repay additional costs makes the commitment open-ended.

27. **10:38 — "he himself entered" (αυτος εισηλθεν):** Sinaiticus reads αυτος εισηλθεν εις κωμην τινα. The village is unnamed in Sinaiticus. From John 11:1 we learn that Martha and Mary lived in Bethany, near Jerusalem. Its placement here in Luke's Travel Narrative (which begins at 9:51) creates a literary tension, as Jesus is supposedly still en route to Jerusalem.

28. **10:39 — "having sat at the feet of the Lord" (παρακαθεσθεισα προς τους ποδας του κυριου):** Sinaiticus reads παρακαθεσθεισα, an aorist passive participle from παρακαθεζομαι ("to sit beside/at"). Sitting at someone's feet was the posture of a disciple learning from a rabbi (cf. Acts 22:3, "at the feet of Gamaliel"). Mary assumes the posture of a Torah student — remarkable for a woman in first-century Judaism.

29. **10:40 — "was being distracted" (περιεσπατο):** Sinaiticus reads περιεσπατο, the imperfect passive of περισπαω ("to draw around/distract"). The prefix περι- ("around") suggests being pulled in multiple directions simultaneously. The imperfect tense indicates an ongoing state of distraction, not a single moment.

30. **10:41 — "Martha, Martha" (Μαρθα Μαρθα):** Sinaiticus reads the doubled vocative Μαρθα Μαρθα. The repetition of a name in direct address is a Lukan pattern (cf. 22:31 "Simon, Simon"; Acts 9:4 "Saul, Saul"). It conveys both tenderness and urgency — a gentle rebuke from intimate concern.

31. **10:42 — "one [thing] is needful" (ενος δε εστιν χρεια):** Sinaiticus reads ενος δε εστιν χρεια. This is a contested variant. Some manuscripts read ολιγων δε εστιν χρεια η ενος ("few things are needful, or one"), which could refer to simpler meal preparation. The Sinaiticus reading "one thing is needful" is more clearly spiritual — the singular necessity is the word of the Lord that Mary has chosen.

32. **10:42 — "the good part" (την αγαθην μεριδα):** Sinaiticus reads την αγαθην μεριδα ("the good portion/part"). The term μερις can denote a portion of food (cf. Genesis 43:34 LXX) or a share/inheritance (cf. Psalm 16:5 LXX, "the Lord is the portion of my inheritance"). The double meaning may be intentional: while Martha prepares food portions, Mary has chosen the better "portion" — the word of God.
